narrative:
Dear Student,

I understand that receiving a rejection for your university application can be disheartening, and I want to help you understand the reasons behind this decision. The model used to evaluate your application predicted a 46% probability that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This prediction led to the rejection of your application, as the university aims to admit students with a higher likelihood of passing.

Let's look at the factors that influenced this prediction. Your academic performance in Year 1 and Year 3, represented by your decile rankings, were significant contributors. Both of these were lower than the average, which pushed the prediction towards a potential failure. Additionally, your LSAT score, while slightly below the average, also played a role in this direction. Your family income bracket, categorized as lower-middle, was another factor that influenced the prediction towards failure, as it might suggest fewer resources for preparation.

On the positive side, your undergraduate GPA was above average, which worked in your favor by suggesting strong academic capabilities. Your racial background and gender had a relatively minor influence on the prediction. However, being a part-time student, which is less common, slightly contributed to the prediction of failure, possibly due to the challenges of balancing work and study.

The most influential factors were your decile rankings and LSAT score, followed by family income and undergraduate GPA. To improve your chances of passing the bar exam in the future, you might consider focusing on enhancing your LSAT preparation and seeking additional academic support to boost your performance. Additionally, exploring resources or scholarships that could alleviate financial constraints might also be beneficial.

Remember, this prediction is not a definitive measure of your potential. With dedication and the right support, you can work towards achieving your goal of passing the bar exam.
